children.description: Naive oversampling duplicates minority samples and risks overfitting to them; SMOTE synthesizes new minority examples in feature space, improving generalization on rare classes.

children.title: Class Weights and Threshold Adjustment
children.description: Adjusting class weights re-balances gradient updates during training without synthetic data, which is safer when the feature space is sparse; threshold tuning then aligns the decision boundary with business cost ratios.

ml-analyst.title: Model Interpretability and Explainability
ml-analyst.description: Explain model predictions to business stakeholders using SHAP values, partial dependence plots, and feature importance. Translate technical ML outputs into actionable business language.
ml-analyst.challenge: Apply SHAP to explain a trained model, produce a global feature importance summary plot and 3 individual force plots, and write a plain-language explanation for each.

children.title: SHAP Values and Summary Plots
children.description: SHAP values provide theoretically grounded, consistent feature attributions for any model; presenting raw feature importances without SHAP can misrepresent which features actually drive individual predictions.

children.title: Partial Dependence Plots
children.description: PDPs show the marginal effect of one or two features on predicted outcomes, revealing non-linear relationships that are invisible in a single coefficient; misreading them ignores interaction effects that alter the depicted trend.

children.title: LIME for Individual Predictions
children.description: LIME fits a locally interpretable model around a single prediction, explaining why the model made that specific decision; without per-prediction explanations, stakeholders cannot audit individual high-stakes outcomes.

resources.label: LIME Paper: Why Should I Trust You?

children.title: SHAP Force Plots and Waterfall Plots
children.description: Force and waterfall plots show how each feature pushed a single prediction away from the base rate, which is the minimum explanation a business stakeholder needs to act on or challenge a model decision.

children.title: Translating ML Outputs to Business Language
children.description: Technical metrics like AUC and SHAP values are meaningless to business stakeholders; translating them into monetary impact, risk reduction, or decision thresholds drives organizational adoption of model recommendations.

children.title: Model Cards for Transparency
children.description: Model cards document intended use, evaluation results across subgroups, and known limitations, enabling auditors and business users to understand where a model should and should not be trusted.

children.title: Detecting Model Bias Across Groups
children.description: A model with high overall accuracy can systematically underperform for protected groups; auditing performance metrics by demographic segment is the minimum required before deploying any people-facing model.

children.title: SHAP Interaction Values
children.description: Interaction values reveal how pairs of features jointly influence predictions, uncovering conditional relationships that global main-effect plots mask and that often correspond to real business logic.

ml-analyst.title: Unsupervised Analysis and Clustering
ml-analyst.description: Use clustering and dimensionality reduction to find hidden structure in unlabeled data for analytical purposes. Profile clusters in business terms and visualize high-dimensional data.
ml-analyst.challenge: Perform clustering on a behavioral dataset, determine optimal K using silhouette analysis, visualize clusters with PCA, and profile each cluster with business-relevant statistics.

children.title: K-Means and Optimal K Selection
children.description: Choosing K arbitrarily produces clusters that reflect the analyst's assumptions rather than the data's structure; elbow analysis and silhouette scores provide objective criteria for K selection.

children.title: DBSCAN for Density-Based Clustering
children.description: DBSCAN discovers clusters of arbitrary shape and explicitly labels noise points, making it superior to K-Means when clusters are non-spherical or when detecting anomalies matters.

children.title: PCA for Visualization and Preprocessing
children.description: PCA reduces correlated high-dimensional features to orthogonal components, both enabling 2D cluster visualization and removing multicollinearity before training distance-sensitive models.

children.title: t-SNE and UMAP for Exploration
children.description: t-SNE and UMAP preserve local neighborhood structure for visualization, making hidden cluster separations visible; treating their outputs as ground-truth distances leads to false conclusions about cluster relationships.

children.title: Silhouette Analysis
children.description: Silhouette scores measure both intra-cluster cohesion and inter-cluster separation in a single value, giving a more complete picture of clustering quality than the elbow method alone.

children.title: Hierarchical Clustering and Dendrograms
children.description: Dendrograms let you explore the natural hierarchy in data and choose a cut height based on business context, avoiding the need to pre-specify K while revealing nested groupings.

children.title: Business-Oriented Cluster Naming
children.description: Clusters labeled '0, 1, 2' have zero business value; profiling each cluster with mean feature values and translating them into behavioral personas makes insights actionable for marketing and product teams.

resources.label: Seaborn Violin and Box Plot for Profiling

children.title: Stability Analysis for Cluster Validation
children.description: Clusters that change dramatically with small perturbations to the data are artifacts of noise rather than true structure; bootstrapping cluster assignments tests whether segments are stable enough to act on.

ml-analyst.title: Regression Analysis and Forecasting
ml-analyst.description: Apply regression models and time series methods for business forecasting and trend analysis. Validate forecast accuracy and communicate uncertainty with confidence intervals.
ml-analyst.challenge: Build a 12-month sales forecast using both ARIMA and Prophet, compare their MAPE scores on a held-out period, and visualize forecasts with confidence bands.

children.title: Trend, Seasonality, and Residual Decomposition
children.description: Decomposing a series before modeling separates the components a business needs to explain and forecast separately; ignoring seasonality causes ARIMA to absorb it as noise, producing poor forecasts.

children.title: Autocorrelation and Stationarity Tests
children.description: ARIMA requires a stationary series; fitting it to a non-stationary series produces spurious correlations where a random walk appears to predict outcomes just because both are trending.

children.title: ARIMA and SARIMA Modeling
children.description: ARIMA captures autoregressive and moving-average dependencies in stationary series; failing to include seasonal terms (SARIMA) for monthly or quarterly data leaves predictable patterns in residuals.

children.title: Prophet for Business Forecasting
children.description: Prophet's additive model handles business-day seasonality, holidays, and trend changepoints out of the box, making it far more practical than ARIMA for analysts without signal processing backgrounds.

children.title: Walk-Forward Validation for Time Series
children.description: Standard cross-validation shuffles time order, leaking future data into training; walk-forward validation respects causality by always training on past data and evaluating on future data.

children.title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics: MAPE, RMSE, MAE
children.description: MAPE is scale-independent and interpretable as a percentage error, making it the preferred metric for communicating forecast quality to business stakeholders who lack statistical backgrounds.

children.title: Rolling Statistics for Anomaly Flags
children.description: Flagging observations that fall outside rolling mean plus N standard deviations identifies genuine outliers without requiring labeled anomaly data, enabling automated monitoring pipelines.

children.title: Isolation Forest for Multivariate Anomalies
children.description: Isolation Forest identifies anomalies in multivariate time series by measuring how easily a point is isolated from the rest; it scales better than statistical methods when multiple correlated signals must be monitored simultaneously.

ml-analyst.title: Capstone - ML Analysis Project
ml-analyst.description: Deliver a complete ML analysis project from problem definition through model building, interpretation, and stakeholder reporting. Document methodology, findings, and business recommendations.
ml-analyst.challenge: Complete an end-to-end ML analysis: frame a business problem, build and tune a model, explain predictions with SHAP, and deliver a written report with actionable recommendations.

children.title: A/B Test Statistical Foundations
children.description: Running an A/B test without pre-calculating sample size leads to underpowered experiments that declare insignificant results significant or miss real effects, wasting product development cycles.

children.title: Hypothesis Formulation and Null Hypotheses
children.description: A vaguely defined hypothesis leads to cherry-picking metrics after the experiment, massively inflating Type I error; pre-registering the primary metric and null hypothesis forces rigorous thinking before running the test.

resources.label: Evan Miller: How Not to Run an A/B Test

children.title: MLflow for Experiment Logging
children.description: Without experiment tracking, you cannot reliably reproduce a past result or compare the true impact of a change; MLflow logs parameters, metrics, and artifacts so every run is fully auditable.

children.title: Weights and Biases for Run Comparison
children.description: W&B provides interactive dashboards for comparing runs across hyperparameter settings, making it easy to identify which configuration drove a performance gain rather than guessing from flat log files.

children.title: Multiple Testing Correction
children.description: Testing 20 metrics simultaneously gives a 64% chance of a false positive at alpha=0.05; Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg correction keeps the experiment-wide error rate under control.

children.title: Bayesian A/B Testing
children.description: Frequentist tests require waiting for a pre-specified sample size; Bayesian approaches provide probability-of-superiority estimates at any sample size, enabling faster decisions with explicit uncertainty quantification.

resources.label: Scipy Beta Distribution for Bayesian AB

children.title: Cohen's d and Effect Size Interpretation
children.description: A statistically significant result with a tiny effect size may not justify a product change; effect size translates statistical findings into practical business impact, separating meaningful changes from noise.

children.title: Confidence Intervals for Business Reporting
children.description: Reporting a point estimate without confidence intervals gives decision-makers false precision; CIs communicate the range of plausible outcomes and let leaders weigh the risk of acting on an experiment result.

data-architect.title: Data Architecture Foundations
data-architect.description: Understand the scope of the data architect role and the layers of a modern enterprise data platform. Learn to diagram systems, evaluate patterns, and speak the language of data infrastructure.
data-architect.challenge: Draw a current-state and future-state architecture diagram for a fictional company migrating from on-prem databases to a cloud data platform.

children.title: Operational vs. Analytical Systems
children.description: Conflating OLTP and OLAP systems leads to catastrophic performance failures — transactional databases crumble under analytical query loads. Understanding where each fits in a data platform is the first architectural decision every data architect must get right.

children.title: Data Platform Layers
children.description: A production data platform spans ingestion, storage, transformation, serving, and governance layers. Skipping or merging layers creates brittle systems where a schema change in one source cascades into broken dashboards across the business.

children.title: Architecture Diagrams with draw.io
children.description: Architecture diagrams are the primary artifact that aligns engineers, stakeholders, and executives on what is being built and why. An ambiguous or missing diagram is a leading cause of misaligned implementations that cost months to unwind.

children.title: Data Governance Overview
children.description: Governance is not an afterthought — retrofitting ownership, lineage, and quality standards onto an ungoverned platform is one of the most expensive and disruptive projects a data organization can undertake. Architects must build governance hooks in from day one.

children.title: What is a Data Architect?
children.description: The data architect owns the blueprints of the entire data ecosystem — deciding how data is structured, stored, moved, and consumed across the organization. Without a clear architectural owner, systems grow organically into an unmaintainable tangle of point-to-point integrations.

children.title: Current-State vs. Future-State Architecture
children.description: Migration projects fail when teams skip documenting the current state before designing the target state — unknown dependencies surface mid-migration and derail timelines. A rigorous as-is/to-be analysis is the foundation of any credible data platform roadmap.

children.title: Speaking the Language of Data Infrastructure
children.description: Data architects who cannot translate technical trade-offs into business terms lose executive buy-in for the foundational investments that make platforms reliable. Fluency in both domains — latency budgets, SLA guarantees, cost-per-query — is a non-negotiable skill.

resources.label: Data Architecture: A Primer for the Data Scientist

children.title: Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)
children.description: Decisions made without documentation are silently reversed by new team members who rediscover the same constraints months later. ADRs capture the context, options considered, and rationale so future architects understand why the system looks the way it does.

data-architect.title: Relational Database Design and SQL
data-architect.description: Design normalized relational schemas and write the advanced SQL needed to query and transform data across complex table structures. Master indexing and query performance fundamentals.
data-architect.challenge: Design and implement a 3NF schema for an e-commerce system, add appropriate indexes, and write a EXPLAIN ANALYZE query demonstrating index usage.

children.title: Third Normal Form (3NF) Normalization
children.description: Under-normalized schemas harbor update anomalies that silently corrupt data — a customer name stored in 12 tables creates 12 places where an update can fail. 3NF eliminates transitive dependencies and is the baseline for any OLTP system that must maintain data integrity.

children.title: Primary Keys, Foreign Keys, and Referential Integrity
children.description: Foreign key constraints are the database's enforcement layer for relational logic — without them, orphaned rows accumulate and downstream joins silently drop or multiply rows, producing numbers that no one trusts. Defining key relationships at the schema level prevents entire classes of data quality bugs.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke — SQL Indexing Guide

children.title: Index Types and Usage
children.description: Choosing the wrong index type — or having no index at all — turns a millisecond lookup into a full table scan that blocks production workloads. Understanding B-tree, hash, and partial indexes and when each applies is essential for designing schemas that stay fast at scale.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke — Anatomy of an Index

children.title: EXPLAIN ANALYZE and Query Plans
children.description: Query performance problems are invisible without execution plan analysis — a plan showing sequential scans on million-row tables exposes the indexes that are missing or being bypassed. Every data architect must read query plans fluently before signing off on a schema design.

children.title: Advanced Joins and Set Operations
children.description: Misapplied join types — using an inner join where a left join is required — silently drop records and produce subtly wrong aggregates that pass visual inspection. Mastering all join types and their edge cases is required to write SQL that correctly reflects business logic.

resources.label: SQL Window Functions Guide — Mode Analytics

children.title: Table Partitioning
children.description: Partitioning is the primary mechanism for keeping queries fast on tables that grow without bound — a query on a 10-year fact table that cannot prune partitions scans 10x more data than necessary. Architects must design partition strategies at table creation time because retrofitting partitioning is painful and expensive.

children.title: Transactions and ACID Properties
children.description: Systems that skip proper transaction isolation produce race conditions that corrupt balances, counts, and audit trails in ways that are extremely hard to reproduce and debug. Every architect designing a write path must understand atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability and choose the right isolation level.

children.title: Schema Migration and Versioning
children.description: Unmanaged schema changes applied directly to production databases are a leading cause of outages and data loss — there is no rollback when a column is accidentally dropped. Schema migration tools like Flyway and Liquibase bring CI/CD discipline to database changes.

data-architect.title: Dimensional Modeling and Data Warehousing
data-architect.description: Design dimensional models that balance query performance with maintainability for analytical workloads. Apply Kimball methodology to real business domains.
data-architect.challenge: Design a full dimensional model for a telecom company including subscriber, service, and billing dimensions with appropriate SCD types documented.

children.title: Star Schema Design
children.description: A poorly designed star schema forces analysts to write complex multi-join queries to answer simple business questions, slowing every report and increasing the probability of wrong answers. The star schema's denormalized dimension tables are specifically optimized for the read patterns of analytical workloads.

children.title: Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCDs)
children.description: Choosing the wrong SCD type erases the historical record that businesses need for compliance and trend analysis — a Type 1 overwrite on a customer address makes it impossible to know where packages were shipped 6 months ago. Every dimension attribute must have an explicit SCD policy designed before data starts flowing.

children.title: Data Mart vs. Enterprise Data Warehouse
children.description: Building data marts without an enterprise warehouse creates siloed, inconsistent metrics — the finance mart and the sales mart use different customer counts and neither team trusts the other's numbers. The enterprise warehouse provides conformed dimensions that make mart numbers reconcilable.

children.title: Conformed Dimensions
children.description: Dimensions that are not conformed across subject areas make it impossible to drill across fact tables — you cannot combine web analytics and CRM data without a shared definition of a customer. Conformed dimensions are the architectural foundation of cross-functional reporting.

resources.label: dbt Metrics and Semantic Layer Docs

children.title: Fact Table Grain
children.description: An undefined or inconsistent grain is the single most common dimensional modeling mistake — mixing daily and monthly measurements in the same fact table produces double-counts and wrong averages that are nearly impossible to debug after the fact. Every fact table must have one declared, atomic grain and every row must conform to it.

children.title: Factless Fact Tables and Aggregate Tables
children.description: Factless fact tables capture the existence of events — student enrollment, promotional coverage — that have no natural numeric measure but must be counted and filtered. Aggregate fact tables pre-summarize high-volume data to deliver sub-second dashboard performance without expensive repeated full-table scans.

children.title: BigQuery — Schema and Partitioning for Analytics
children.description: BigQuery charges by bytes scanned, so a dimensional model without date partitioning and appropriate clustering turns every analytical query into an unbounded cost that scales with data growth. Cloud warehouses demand cost-aware schema design that is different from on-premises dimensional modeling.

children.title: Snowflake — Clustering and Micro-Partition Optimization
children.description: Snowflake's automatic clustering can become misaligned with query patterns as data grows, causing full micro-partition scans on large fact tables. Architects must understand cluster keys and when to trigger a manual reclustering to maintain query performance at scale.

data-architect.title: Cloud Data Platforms - Snowflake and BigQuery
data-architect.description: Configure and administer cloud data warehouses including compute scaling, storage organization, and cost controls. Understand the architectural differences between Snowflake and BigQuery.
data-architect.challenge: Load a 10GB dataset into both Snowflake and BigQuery, configure appropriate clustering/partitioning on each, and document the cost and performance differences.

children.title: Virtual Warehouses and Compute Scaling
children.description: A misconfigured virtual warehouse — too small for the workload or auto-suspend disabled — either starves analysts of compute or generates a bill that shocks the finance team. Understanding how Snowflake separates compute from storage and how to right-size virtual warehouses is the core cost-control skill on the platform.

children.title: Snowflake Storage Architecture and Data Loading
children.description: Snowflake stores data in internally optimized columnar micro-partitions that are invisible to users — loading data in the wrong format or without compression wastes both storage and compute during query execution. Architects must understand staging areas, file formats, and COPY INTO patterns to design efficient ingestion pipelines.

resources.label: Snowflake Stages and File Formats Docs

children.title: BigQuery Slots and Reservations
children.description: On-demand BigQuery pricing can produce unpredictable bills when analysts run large exploratory queries — a single unguarded full-table scan on a petabyte dataset can cost hundreds of dollars. Reservations and slot commitments convert BigQuery costs from variable to predictable, which is required for enterprise budget management.

children.title: BigQuery IAM and Access Control
children.description: BigQuery's column-level security and row-level access policies are the only mechanisms that enforce data classification compliance without duplicating tables — missing them means PII is visible to every analyst by default. Architects must design the IAM taxonomy before any data lands in production.

children.title: Architectural Differences: Snowflake vs. BigQuery
children.description: Choosing the wrong cloud warehouse for an organization's workload profile locks in years of technical debt — Snowflake's per-second compute model favors concurrent mixed workloads while BigQuery's serverless model favors bursty, large-scale analytical queries. Architects must map workload characteristics to platform strengths before committing.

resources.label: Snowflake vs BigQuery Comparison — Fivetran Blog

children.title: Amazon Redshift Architecture
children.description: Redshift's MPP architecture distributes data across nodes using distribution keys — a poor distribution key choice creates data skew where one node handles 80% of the work, making every query slow regardless of cluster size. Architects evaluating AWS-native stacks must understand Redshift's distribution and sort key design.

resources.label: Redshift Best Practices for Table Design

children.title: Cost Monitoring and Chargebacks
children.description: Without cost attribution, cloud warehouse spend grows unchecked until finance raises an emergency — teams that cannot see their query costs have no incentive to write efficient SQL. Architects must instrument spend dashboards and implement per-team resource monitors before the platform is opened for general use.

children.title: Snowflake Role-Based Access Control
children.description: Snowflake's RBAC is additive — roles inherit all privileges of roles they are granted — so a poorly designed role hierarchy accidentally grants broad data access to users who should see only a subset. Architects must design the role hierarchy before onboarding users to avoid a painful remediation effort.

data-architect.title: Data Transformation with dbt
data-architect.description: Design dbt project structures that reflect a well-governed transformation layer, with clear staging, intermediate, and mart conventions. Apply dbt patterns for large, multi-domain projects.
data-architect.challenge: Design a multi-domain dbt project structure for a company with sales, finance, and operations data; implement the staging layer for one domain with full tests and docs.

children.title: Staging, Intermediate, and Mart Layers
children.description: A dbt project without clear layer conventions forces analysts to query raw source tables directly, bypassing the transformation logic that ensures data quality — any source schema change breaks every downstream report without warning. The staging/intermediate/mart separation creates a contractual interface between sources and consumers.

resources.label: dbt — How We Structure Our dbt Projects

children.title: Materializations at Scale
children.description: Using the wrong materialization strategy — running an incremental model as a full refresh on a billion-row table — can turn a 2-minute dbt run into a 4-hour job that blocks the entire transformation pipeline. Architects must match materialization type to data volume, update frequency, and query cost for each model.

resources.label: dbt Incremental Models — When to Use

children.title: dbt Tests — Uniqueness, Not-Null, and Custom
children.description: Untested dbt models silently propagate duplicate and null keys that corrupt joins and aggregations throughout the downstream pipeline — by the time the issue surfaces in a dashboard, it is impossible to determine which transformation introduced the bug. A test at every layer acts as a contract that quarantines data quality failures at their source.

children.title: dbt Docs and Lineage Graph
children.description: Without generated documentation, institutional knowledge about what each model means and who owns it lives only in the heads of people who may leave the organization. dbt docs auto-generates a queryable lineage graph and data dictionary from YAML descriptions that architects maintain alongside the code.

children.title: Multi-Project dbt Design with Mesh
children.description: A monolithic dbt project with hundreds of models belonging to different business domains becomes impossible to maintain and deploy independently — a bug fix in the finance domain triggers a full rebuild of models owned by engineering teams who have no context on what changed. dbt Mesh decentralizes ownership by allowing cross-project references with explicit public interfaces.

children.title: dbt Packages and Reusability
children.description: Duplicating transformation logic across projects creates a maintenance nightmare — a business rule change must be applied everywhere by hand, and one missed site produces conflicting metric definitions across the organization. dbt packages allow shared macros, models, and tests to be versioned and reused across projects.

children.title: Model Contracts and Versioning
children.description: When a widely-consumed dbt model changes its schema without a versioning contract, every downstream consumer breaks simultaneously — a fact table column rename can take an analytics team days to remediate. dbt model contracts declare column types and constraints that must pass before a model can be deployed, making breaking changes explicit.

children.title: Jinja Macros for Reusable Logic
children.description: SQL logic repeated across dozens of models — currency conversion, date truncation, PII masking — becomes inconsistent when each instance is maintained separately. Jinja macros in dbt compile the same logic into every model at build time, guaranteeing a single source of truth for business rules applied in SQL.

data-architect.title: Data Governance and Metadata Management
data-architect.description: Implement data governance frameworks including data cataloging, lineage tracking, ownership assignment, and data classification. Ensure regulatory and compliance requirements are met.
data-architect.challenge: Implement a data catalog for a sample warehouse using dbt docs plus a metadata layer; classify all columns by sensitivity level and assign business owners.

children.title: Business Glossary and Data Definitions
children.description: Without a shared business glossary, the word 'revenue' means something different in every team's report — the finance team's number never matches the sales team's number and trust in data collapses. A curated glossary is the single source of truth for term definitions that all metric calculations must reference.

children.title: Data Lineage Tracking
children.description: When a source table changes unexpectedly, a team with no lineage graph spends hours manually tracing which downstream models, reports, and dashboards are affected — a team with lineage sees the impact in seconds. Automated lineage is a prerequisite for safe, confident schema evolution in any production data platform.

children.title: PII Classification and Sensitivity Tagging
children.description: Unclassified data creates unlimited compliance liability — PII sitting in unmasked columns of a shared analytics workspace can result in GDPR fines measured in percentages of annual global revenue. Data architects must implement a classification taxonomy and enforce it with automated tagging at ingestion time.

children.title: Data Quality SLAs and Monitoring
children.description: Data quality issues discovered by business users in dashboards cost 10x more to remediate than issues caught at the pipeline level — by then, decisions have already been made on bad data. Defining quality SLAs and enforcing them with automated monitors at each pipeline stage is the architect's responsibility.

children.title: DAMA DMBOK and Governance Domains
children.description: Organizations that adopt governance practices without a framework often implement inconsistent policies that create compliance theater rather than real controls. The DMBOK provides a complete map of governance domains — from data quality to master data management — that architects use to prioritize and sequence governance programs.

resources.label: AWS Data Governance on AWS Whitepaper

children.title: Data Ownership and Stewardship
children.description: Data without an assigned owner is data nobody is accountable for — quality degrades, documentation goes stale, and governance reviews have nobody to call when anomalies appear. Every data domain in a governed platform must have a designated business owner and a technical steward with explicit responsibilities.

children.title: Technical Metadata vs. Business Metadata
children.description: Technical metadata alone — table names, column types, row counts — is useless to business analysts who need to know what a table means and whether it is safe to use in a regulatory report. Architects must design catalog systems that bridge technical and business metadata so every dataset has both machine-readable schema and human-readable context.

children.title: Schema Registry for Streaming Data
children.description: In event-driven architectures, a producer that changes an event schema without coordinating with consumers silently breaks all downstream consumers that expect the old format. A schema registry enforces compatibility rules and version negotiation before any schema change is allowed to propagate.

data-architect.title: Data Security and Compliance
data-architect.description: Design and enforce data security controls including column-level masking, row-level access policies, and audit logging for compliance with GDPR and SOC 2. Apply least-privilege access models.
data-architect.challenge: Implement dynamic data masking on PII columns in Snowflake, configure row-level access by department, and demonstrate with a multi-role query test.

children.title: Dynamic Data Masking and Column-Level Security
children.description: Static copies of masked data go stale and multiply the attack surface — a breach of the anonymized copy still exposes sensitive data if the masking was reversible. Dynamic masking applies redaction at query time based on role, so a single governed copy of the data serves both privileged and unprivileged users safely.

children.title: Row-Level Access Policies
children.description: Without row-level security, a sales analyst can query revenue data for competitors' accounts simply by removing a WHERE clause — the only safeguard is the analyst's self-discipline. Row access policies enforce data visibility rules at the storage layer so they cannot be bypassed regardless of how the query is written.

children.title: GDPR and CCPA Compliance Patterns
children.description: GDPR's right-to-erasure requirement is technically incompatible with immutable append-only data stores unless the architecture explicitly designs for it — tombstone records, pseudonymization, and key destruction are the common patterns. Architects who treat compliance as an afterthought face costly retroactive redesigns and regulatory exposure.

children.title: Audit Logging and Monitoring
children.description: A data breach that cannot be traced to its source because audit logs were not enabled turns a manageable security incident into an unquantified liability — regulators require proof of who accessed what data and when. Immutable audit trails are a compliance requirement for SOC 2, HIPAA, and GDPR-regulated platforms.

children.title: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) Design
children.description: Overly permissive roles granted in haste during onboarding compound over time into an access sprawl where most users have far more data access than their job requires — violating least-privilege and creating insider threat risk. Architects must design a role taxonomy before any user is onboarded and review it quarterly.

children.title: Single Sign-On and MFA Integration
children.description: Data warehouses with local usernames and passwords bypass enterprise identity controls, creating unmanaged accounts that persist after employees leave and cannot be revoked from the central identity provider. SSO integration ensures warehouse access is automatically revoked when an account is deprovisioned in the identity provider.

children.title: Private Connectivity and VPC Integration
children.description: Data warehouses accessible over the public internet expose query traffic to interception and the platform to brute-force attacks — enterprise compliance frameworks typically prohibit public endpoints for systems storing regulated data. Private connectivity via VPC peering, Private Link, or VPN eliminates this exposure.

children.title: Encryption at Rest and in Transit
children.description: Encryption defaults vary by platform — some require explicit configuration of customer-managed keys (CMEK) to satisfy compliance requirements that prohibit cloud-provider-managed keys. Architects must understand the encryption posture of each platform component and document the key management strategy in the security design.

data-architect.title: Data Mesh and Modern Architecture Patterns
data-architect.description: Evaluate and design data mesh architectures that decentralize data ownership to domain teams while maintaining interoperability. Compare with centralized warehouse and data lakehouse patterns.
data-architect.challenge: Design a data mesh architecture for a 3-domain organization, document the data product contracts for one domain, and implement cross-domain sharing in Snowflake.

children.title: Domain Ownership and Data Products
children.description: A centralized data team that owns all pipelines becomes a bottleneck that cannot scale — every new data need requires a ticket to one team, slowing the entire organization. Data mesh transfers ownership to domain teams who build and operate their own data products, turning the central team from a bottleneck into a platform provider.

children.title: Federated Computational Governance
children.description: Decentralizing data ownership without a governance plane produces a proliferation of incompatible schemas, access models, and quality standards — data that cannot be joined across domains because each team invented its own conventions. Federated governance provides the interoperability rules that allow autonomous domains to collaborate.

children.title: Delta Lake and the Lakehouse Pattern
children.description: Pure data lakes without ACID transactions accumulate corrupted and partially-written files that make reliable analytics impossible — a failed pipeline leaves the lake in an indeterminate state that requires manual cleanup. Delta Lake adds transaction logs, schema enforcement, and time-travel to object storage, combining the flexibility of lakes with the reliability of warehouses.

children.title: Apache Iceberg for Large Analytic Tables
children.description: Traditional Hive-style partitioning requires partition columns in every query and breaks on partition evolution — renaming a partition column requires rewriting the entire table. Iceberg's hidden partitioning and partition evolution allow architects to change partition strategies without rewriting data or breaking existing queries.

children.title: Snowflake Secure Data Sharing
children.description: Sharing data by copying files or exporting CSVs creates stale copies that diverge from the source and cannot be access-controlled after distribution. Snowflake's secure data sharing lets providers share live, governed data without data movement — the consumer always queries the provider's current data under the provider's access policies.

children.title: BigQuery Analytics Hub
children.description: Publishing datasets to external partners via direct access grants gives partners access to the entire dataset without granular controls. Analytics Hub provides a governed exchange layer where publishers list specific dataset versions with access controls and subscribers receive a zero-copy linked dataset they cannot accidentally over-access.

children.title: Terraform for Cloud Data Infrastructure
children.description: Manually creating warehouses, databases, and IAM roles through a UI produces infrastructure that cannot be reproduced, version-controlled, or audited — the exact configuration exists only in one cloud console and any mistake requires manual rollback. Terraform codifies infrastructure as reviewed, tested, and versioned artifacts.

children.title: GitOps for Data Platform Changes
children.description: Infrastructure changes applied manually in production without peer review are the leading cause of configuration drift — over time, what the code says diverges from what is actually running. A GitOps workflow requires every infrastructure change to pass through a pull request, CI validation, and automated apply, making every change auditable.

data-architect.title: Performance, Scalability, and Cost Optimization
data-architect.description: Diagnose and resolve performance bottlenecks in cloud data platforms using query profiling, materialization strategies, and storage optimization. Design for cost-efficient scale.
data-architect.challenge: Profile a slow reporting query, apply clustering, materialized views, and query rewrite techniques to reduce runtime by 60%, and document cost savings.

children.title: Execution Plans and Query Profiling
children.description: Slow queries with no visible bottleneck are impossible to fix without reading the execution plan — the difference between a 30-second query and a 2-second query is often a single missed partition prune visible only in the profile. Query profiling is the diagnostic skill that separates architects who guess at performance from those who know what to fix.

children.title: Materialized Views and Result Caching
children.description: Dashboard queries that recompute the same aggregation on billions of rows every 30 seconds consume compute budgets that scale linearly with dashboard adoption — without pre-aggregation, more users means proportionally more cost. Materialized views and result caches decouple query cost from user count for stable, predictable workloads.

children.title: Clustering Key Selection
children.description: A clustering key chosen for the wrong column — one with high cardinality but never filtered in queries — provides no partition pruning benefit while adding write overhead and reclustering cost. Clustering key design must be driven by the actual filter patterns of the highest-cost queries, not by assumptions.

children.title: Data Compression and File Format Selection
children.description: Storing data in row-oriented formats like CSV in a columnar query engine means the engine reads every column to answer a query that references only two — wasting up to 90% of IO bandwidth. Columnar formats like Parquet and ORC with Snappy or Zstd compression reduce both storage costs and query times by an order of magnitude.

children.title: Cost Allocation and Chargeback Models
children.description: When all warehouse costs are pooled in a single account, there is no signal to identify which teams or workloads are responsible for cost spikes — engineering can spend hours investigating a bill with no attribution. Tag-based cost allocation and department-level resource monitors create accountability that drives efficient query behavior.

children.title: Snowflake Query Acceleration Service
children.description: Large, unpredictable analytical queries that cannot be pre-materialized cause virtual warehouse queuing that blocks interactive workloads from responding in time. The Query Acceleration Service offloads parts of eligible queries to shared serverless compute, smoothing latency spikes without permanently upsizing the virtual warehouse.

children.title: Multi-Cluster Warehouses for Concurrency
children.description: A single-cluster virtual warehouse that queues 50 concurrent dashboard refresh queries adds seconds of latency to every user interaction during peak hours — the problem gets worse the more the platform succeeds. Multi-cluster warehouses automatically add compute nodes under concurrency load and remove them when demand drops, keeping latency flat as adoption grows.

children.title: BigQuery BI Engine for Sub-Second Dashboards
children.description: Standard BigQuery query latency of 1-5 seconds is too slow for interactive dashboard filters that business users expect to respond instantly. BI Engine caches frequently-accessed data in memory and answers eligible queries without spinning up compute, delivering sub-second response times at a fraction of the cost of repeated full-table scans.

data-architect.title: Capstone - Enterprise Data Platform Design
data-architect.description: Deliver a full enterprise data platform design including architecture diagrams, dimensional models, transformation layer, governance framework, and security controls. Present to a simulated leadership team.
data-architect.challenge: Design and partially implement an enterprise data platform: produce architecture diagrams, a dimensional model, a dbt project with 2 marts, a governance catalog, and a 15-slide executive presentation.

children.title: End-to-End Data Flow Design
children.description: An enterprise data platform without a documented end-to-end data flow is a system no new team member can understand or safely modify — changes are made by reverse-engineering production and hoping nothing breaks. The capstone deliverable must trace every data domain from source to consumption layer, making the entire system navigable.

children.title: Architecture Decision Records for Platform Choices
children.description: Platform decisions made without documented rationale are silently reversed by future architects who rediscover the same constraints — a team that chose Snowflake over BigQuery for a well-reasoned cost model will see that decision re-litigated every quarter if the rationale was never written down. ADRs are the durable memory of the architecture.

children.title: Governance Catalog Implementation
children.description: A governance program that exists only in a policy document but has no tooling implementation provides no actual protection — PII is still exposed, lineage is still unknown, and quality SLAs are still unmeasured. The capstone must include a working catalog with classified columns, documented owners, and at least one automated quality check.

children.title: Cost Estimate and Trade-off Analysis
children.description: Architecture proposals without cost estimates are rejected by finance and ignored by executives — a technically perfect solution that is 5x over budget will never be approved. Architects must produce a defensible cost model that shows compute, storage, and license costs at current scale and projected 3-year growth.

children.title: Executive Presentation of Technical Architecture
children.description: Architecture presentations that lead with technical diagrams lose executive audiences within minutes — finance and product executives need to understand business outcomes, risk reduction, and cost impact before they care about micro-partition optimization. Architects who translate technical decisions into business terms unlock the funding and organizational support that platforms need.

resources.label: Gartner — Communicating IT Strategy to the Business

children.title: Platform Roadmap and Phased Delivery
children.description: A complete enterprise platform delivered in a single 18-month big-bang project is almost certain to fail — requirements change, priorities shift, and a system nobody is using for 18 months provides zero business value during that time. Phasing the delivery into quarterly milestones with usable increments keeps stakeholders engaged and allows early course correction.

children.title: dbt Project Structure and Best Practices
children.description: A dbt project without documented conventions is interpreted differently by every contributor — model names, test coverage standards, and YAML documentation requirements drift until the project becomes inconsistent and ungoverned. The capstone dbt project must include a CONTRIBUTING guide that codifies every architectural convention.

children.title: Snowflake Architecture Reference Guide
children.description: Without a reference architecture document, every new team member must reverse-engineer the platform configuration from scratch — VWH naming conventions, RBAC hierarchy, and database organization are invisible until someone explains them. A reference guide reduces onboarding time from weeks to days and prevents configuration drift caused by undocumented conventions.

analytics-engineer.title: Analytics Engineering Role and SQL Foundations
analytics-engineer.description: Understand where analytics engineering sits between data engineering and data analysis, and the responsibilities it owns. Build the SQL foundation required for robust data transformation work.
analytics-engineer.challenge: Write a SQL query using CTEs that joins 3 tables, computes customer lifetime value, and ranks customers into deciles.

children.title: Modern Data Stack Context
children.description: Analytics engineering sits between data engineering and business intelligence, owning the transformation layer. Without understanding this boundary, you risk duplicating work with engineers or delivering raw, untrusted data directly to analysts.

children.title: Analytics Engineer vs Data Engineer
children.description: Knowing which responsibilities belong to each role prevents costly overlap and ensures SLAs are owned clearly. Misalignment here leads to untransformed data landing in dashboards or transformation logic duplicated across pipelines.

children.title: JOINs and Relationships
children.description: Understanding inner, left, and anti joins is prerequisite to any transformation work. A misapplied join silently fans out rows or drops records, producing subtly wrong aggregations that stakeholders will trust for months.

children.title: CTEs and Subqueries
children.description: Common Table Expressions make complex queries readable and testable by breaking them into named logical steps. Skipping CTEs forces monolithic queries that are hard to debug, review, and maintain in a team environment.

children.title: Warehouse Architecture Overview
children.description: Cloud warehouses like BigQuery, Snowflake, and Redshift separate storage and compute, changing query cost and optimization strategies compared to traditional RDBMS. Ignoring this leads to expensive full-table scans and misunderstood billing.

children.title: Schemas and Namespacing
children.description: Organizing warehouse objects into schemas by data domain and transformation stage creates clear data ownership and access control boundaries. Without schema conventions, warehouses become a flat, ungoverned dumping ground.

children.title: Aggregation Functions
children.description: SUM, COUNT, AVG, and their DISTINCT variants are the building blocks of every KPI. Misunderstanding NULL handling in aggregations or double-counting from joins is the most common source of incorrect business metrics.

children.title: HAVING vs WHERE
children.description: WHERE filters rows before aggregation while HAVING filters after, and confusing the two produces logically incorrect queries that return wrong group totals. This distinction matters most in revenue and cohort calculations.

analytics-engineer.title: dbt Core Fundamentals
analytics-engineer.description: Set up a dbt project connected to a cloud warehouse and build your first staging and mart models. Understand dbt's compile and run cycle and how ref() creates the lineage graph.
analytics-engineer.challenge: Create a dbt project with a staging layer and one mart model that joins 2 staging models; add not-null and unique tests to the mart primary key.

children.title: Staging and Mart Layers
children.description: A consistent staging-intermediate-mart layering convention ensures every model has a single responsibility and a known grain. Skipping this layer structure leads to business logic scattered across uncoordinated models with no clear lineage.

children.title: ref() and the Lineage Graph
children.description: ref() is how dbt builds the dependency graph that enables correct ordering, partial runs, and deferred execution. Using raw table names instead of ref() breaks the DAG and silently causes downstream models to read stale data.

children.title: Table vs View vs Incremental
children.description: Choosing the wrong materialization wastes warehouse compute or serves stale data. Views recompute on every query, tables persist results, and incremental models process only new rows — each trade-off directly impacts cost and freshness SLAs.

children.title: dbt run and dbt test Commands
children.description: The core CLI commands compile Jinja SQL, execute models in dependency order, and run data quality tests. Understanding flags like --select, --exclude, and --full-refresh is essential to running only what's needed without unnecessary warehouse cost.

children.title: Declaring Sources in dbt
children.description: Defining sources in YAML decouples your models from raw table names and enables freshness assertions, making ingestion failures visible before they propagate downstream. Raw table references without source declarations mean dbt has no awareness of external data health.

children.title: Schema YAML and Column Documentation
children.description: YAML files are where tests, column descriptions, and metadata live alongside model definitions. Without them, data consumers have no in-warehouse documentation and tests cannot be applied, turning the warehouse into an undocumented black box.

children.title: Jinja Basics for SQL
children.description: dbt uses Jinja to make SQL dynamic: variables, if-else conditionals, and for-loops let you write DRY models that adapt across environments and configurations. Without Jinja fluency you will copy-paste logic instead of centralizing it.

children.title: dbt Macros
children.description: Macros are reusable Jinja functions that eliminate SQL duplication across models, enforce naming standards, and wrap adapter-specific syntax. Teams that skip macros end up with fragile, warehouse-specific SQL scattered through dozens of model files.

analytics-engineer.title: Advanced SQL for Analytics
analytics-engineer.description: Write the advanced SQL patterns used in production analytics engineering: window functions, pivoting, date spines, and complex aggregations. Apply these inside dbt models.
analytics-engineer.challenge: Build a dbt model that generates a date spine and joins it with event data to compute a daily active user metric with a 7-day rolling average.

children.title: LAG, LEAD, and Ranking Functions
children.description: Window functions compute values across a set of rows related to the current row without collapsing them, enabling period-over-period comparisons and rankings that are impossible with GROUP BY alone. Misunderstanding the PARTITION BY and ORDER BY clause leads to incorrect comparisons across the wrong boundaries.

children.title: Frame Clauses (ROWS vs RANGE)
children.description: ROWS BETWEEN and RANGE BETWEEN control which rows are included in a window frame for rolling calculations like moving averages. Using the default unbounded frame on a cumulative sum produces running totals, not rolling windows, and the silent difference corrupts metrics.

children.title: Generating Date Spines
children.description: A date spine is a continuous sequence of dates used as a join backbone so gaps in event data appear as zero-valued rows rather than missing rows in charts. Without it, daily metrics skip days with no activity and rolling average denominators are wrong.

children.title: Sessionization and Funnel Logic
children.description: Sessionizing event streams requires assigning session IDs based on inactivity thresholds using LAG and conditional window logic. Incorrect sessionization leads to inflated session counts and broken funnel conversion rates that misdirect product decisions.

resources.label: Funnel Analysis with SQL - Locally Optimistic

children.title: PIVOT and UNPIVOT Patterns
children.description: Pivoting transforms row-per-metric data into column-per-metric format required by many BI tools, and unpivoting does the reverse for normalization. Getting the pivot grain wrong produces NULL columns or merged rows that appear to have data when they do not.

children.title: Recursive CTEs
children.description: Recursive CTEs process hierarchical data like org charts and category trees by iterating over parent-child relationships until no new rows are generated. Missing a cycle guard or misunderstanding the anchor-and-recursive structure causes infinite loops or incomplete hierarchy traversals.

children.title: BigQuery-Specific Functions
children.description: BigQuery has unique functions for arrays, structs, and nested data that standard SQL lacks. Flattening nested repeated records requires UNNEST and CROSS JOIN UNNEST, and misusing them fans out rows or loses data that lives inside nested fields.

children.title: Snowflake Semi-Structured Data
children.description: Snowflake's VARIANT type and colon-dot path syntax enable querying JSON columns without pre-flattening, while FLATTEN handles arrays. Ignoring Snowflake-specific semi-structured operators forces expensive upstream transformations that are better handled natively.

analytics-engineer.title: dbt Testing, Documentation, and Data Contracts
analytics-engineer.description: Implement a comprehensive testing strategy across dbt model layers to enforce data quality guarantees for downstream consumers. Write dbt documentation that serves as a living data dictionary.
analytics-engineer.challenge: Add a full test suite to an existing dbt project: schema tests on all primary keys, a singular test checking a business rule, and freshness assertions on all sources.

children.title: Schema Tests (unique, not-null, accepted-values)
children.description: Built-in schema tests are the first line of defense that catch grain violations, null primary keys, and out-of-domain values before models reach production. Skipping these on primary and foreign keys allows silently duplicated or orphaned rows to corrupt downstream reports.

children.title: Singular SQL Tests
children.description: Singular tests are custom SQL queries that return rows only when a business rule is violated, enabling precise assertions that generic tests cannot express. Without them, complex cross-table constraints like referential integrity between marts go untested.

children.title: Source Freshness Assertions
children.description: dbt source freshness checks query a loaded-at timestamp column and fail the run if data is older than a defined threshold, alerting the team before stale data reaches dashboards. Without freshness gates, pipelines appear healthy while silently serving yesterday's numbers as today's.

children.title: Custom Generic Tests
children.description: Custom generic tests package reusable test logic into parameterized macros that can be applied across many models with a single YAML line. Teams without them repeat identical singular tests across dozens of models, making test maintenance impractical at scale.

children.title: dbt docs generate and serve
children.description: dbt generates a data catalog website from model YAML descriptions and the lineage graph, giving every stakeholder a searchable, version-controlled data dictionary. Without generated docs, institutional knowledge lives only in Slack threads and spreadsheets that quickly become outdated.

children.title: Column-Level Descriptions
children.description: Column descriptions in YAML propagate into the generated catalog and can be inherited by downstream models using doc blocks, ensuring definitions stay consistent. Missing column documentation forces every consumer to reverse-engineer business meaning from raw SQL logic.

children.title: dbt Contracts for Model Schemas
children.description: Data contracts enforce that a model always produces a defined set of columns with declared data types, preventing breaking schema changes from silently propagating to downstream consumers. Without contracts, a column rename or type change in a mart can crash a BI dashboard with no pre-flight warning.

children.title: Great Expectations Integration
children.description: Great Expectations extends dbt-native testing with statistical checks like column value distributions, range assertions, and row count thresholds that catch data drift before it reaches consumers. Relying only on schema tests misses distributional anomalies that schema alone cannot detect.

analytics-engineer.title: Looker and LookML Fundamentals
analytics-engineer.description: Define a semantic layer in Looker using LookML views, dimensions, and measures that enable self-service analytics for business users. Understand Explores and the Looker data modeling philosophy.
analytics-engineer.challenge: Build a LookML view with at least 8 dimensions and 4 measures, join two views in an Explore, and create a 3-tile Looker dashboard.

children.title: LookML Views and Fields
children.description: LookML views map to database tables and define dimensions and measures as reusable field definitions that all dashboards consume. Without a well-structured view layer, business users write inconsistent SQL in ad-hoc Looker queries, producing metrics that contradict each other.

children.title: Dimensions vs Measures
children.description: Dimensions are attributes used for grouping and filtering while measures are aggregations, and conflating them breaks the automatic SQL generation Looker performs. Placing aggregation logic in a dimension causes Looker to wrap it incorrectly, producing GROUP BY errors or silent double-aggregation.

children.title: Defining Explores
children.description: An Explore defines which views and joins are available for a self-service analysis session, controlling the scope of what users can query. Overloaded Explores with too many joins produce slow queries and confuse users, while under-scoped Explores force analysts to request new ones for every use case.

children.title: Derived Tables in LookML
children.description: Derived tables let you define a SQL query or PDT (Persistent Derived Table) as a virtual view inside Looker without materializing it in dbt, filling gaps in the semantic layer for BI-layer calculations. Overusing derived tables instead of pushing logic into dbt creates a split data model that is hard to test.

children.title: Building Looker Dashboards
children.description: Looker dashboards compose tiles driven by Explores and LookML-defined fields, so changes to LookML automatically propagate to all consuming dashboards. Dashboards built on ad-hoc Looker queries instead of LookML fields break on schema changes and cannot be maintained centrally.

children.title: Looker Filters and Parameters
children.description: Liquid-templated filters and Looker parameters let users control query scope and generate dynamic SQL conditions directly from the UI. Without them, every stakeholder variation requires a separate dashboard tile, exploding maintenance burden.

children.title: Single Source of Truth Metrics
children.description: The semantic layer ensures that revenue, conversion, and churn are computed identically regardless of which tool or user queries them, eliminating the data credibility crises that arise when Finance and Marketing report different numbers from the same warehouse.

children.title: LookML vs dbt Semantic Layer
children.description: Understanding where Looker's LookML and dbt's MetricFlow semantic layer overlap and differ allows you to choose the right abstraction for each metric definition. Duplicating metric logic in both layers creates drift and requires double-maintenance when business rules change.

analytics-engineer.title: Incremental Models and Performance
analytics-engineer.description: Design and implement incremental dbt models that process only new or changed data to keep warehouse costs low and tables fresh. Understand merge strategies and idempotency.
analytics-engineer.challenge: Convert 3 full-refresh dbt models to incremental using merge strategy; write tests proving no duplicate keys after 5 simulated daily runs.

children.title: Merge and Append Strategies
children.description: Append strategy adds new rows while merge upserts based on a unique key, and choosing the wrong one either duplicates records or prevents historical corrections. A merge model without a correct unique_key silently duplicates rows on every run because dbt cannot identify which rows to update.

children.title: Idempotency and Backfill Patterns
children.description: An idempotent incremental model produces identical results whether run once or many times for the same time window, which is essential for safe backfills and incident recovery. Non-idempotent models accumulate duplicate data on reruns and require manual cleanup that is error-prone at scale.

children.title: Partition Pruning Alignment
children.description: Writing incremental filters that align with warehouse partition columns enables the query optimizer to skip entire partitions, reducing scan cost by orders of magnitude. Filtering on a non-partition column forces full table scans even on incremental runs, negating the cost benefit of incrementality.

children.title: Testing Incremental Models
children.description: Incremental models require tests that verify no duplicate keys accumulate across multiple simulated runs and that late-arriving data is handled correctly. A test suite that only runs full-refresh will never catch the merge logic bugs that emerge during normal incremental execution.

children.title: MERGE Statement in Snowflake
children.description: Snowflake's MERGE statement performs upserts in a single atomic operation and is the engine behind dbt incremental merge strategy. Understanding WHEN MATCHED and WHEN NOT MATCHED clauses helps diagnose why rows are being inserted instead of updated.

children.title: BigQuery Merge and DML Costs
children.description: BigQuery charges for DML operations like MERGE differently than SELECT queries and has specific best-practice limits on how frequently you should run incremental models. Running high-frequency merges on non-partitioned large tables can quickly exhaust budgets with no visible performance gain.

children.title: Partition-Based Delete+Insert
children.description: The delete-insert incremental strategy deletes all rows for the affected date partitions and reinserts them, which is safer than merge for event data where rows can arrive late and need full partition replacement. Using append instead of delete-insert for late-arriving events creates permanent duplicate rows in the affected partitions.

children.title: Cost Impact of Materializations
children.description: Choosing between view, table, and incremental directly controls warehouse compute costs: views pay query cost on every access while tables pay write cost on each run. Materializing every model as a table eliminates recomputation benefits but running heavy transformations as views makes dashboards slow and expensive.

analytics-engineer.title: Airflow for Analytics Orchestration
analytics-engineer.description: Orchestrate dbt project runs and surrounding data tasks with Apache Airflow DAGs. Manage dependencies between ingestion jobs, dbt runs, and downstream exports reliably.
analytics-engineer.challenge: Build an Airflow DAG that runs a data load task, then triggers a dbt run with model selection, sends a Slack alert on failure, and is scheduled daily.

children.title: DAGs, Operators, and Task Dependencies
children.description: An Airflow DAG is a Python script that defines tasks and their dependency relationships using bitshift operators or explicit set_upstream calls. Misdefining task dependencies can cause downstream tasks to run before upstream ones complete, producing empty intermediate tables that fail silently.

children.title: Airflow Scheduling and execution_date
children.description: Airflow's schedule interval and execution_date concept create a time-window-based execution model where each DAG run is associated with a data interval, not a wall-clock run time. Ignoring execution_date when constructing incremental filters means your pipeline processes the wrong time window on every run.

children.title: Running dbt from Airflow
children.description: dbt can be triggered from Airflow using the BashOperator with dbt CLI commands, the Cosmos library for native DAG generation, or the dbt Cloud API operator. Choosing the right integration pattern determines how granular your task-level retry and observability is within the orchestrator.

children.title: dbt Cloud API Triggers
children.description: The dbt Cloud API allows external systems like Airflow to trigger dbt Cloud jobs and poll for completion, enabling hybrid architectures where orchestration and transformation are managed separately. Polling without implementing backoff and timeout logic causes jobs to silently time out in the orchestrator while succeeding in dbt Cloud.

resources.label: Triggering dbt Cloud Jobs via API

children.title: Airflow Retry Configuration
children.description: Configuring retries, retry delays, and retry exponential backoff at the task level ensures transient failures like network blips or warehouse cold starts do not page on-call engineers unnecessarily. Tasks without retries fail immediately on transient errors, triggering false alerts and manual reruns.

children.title: Slack and PagerDuty Alerts from Airflow
children.description: on_failure_callback hooks let you send structured Slack messages or PagerDuty alerts with task name, DAG, execution date, and log URL when a task fails. Without alerting hooks, pipeline failures are only discoverable by manually checking the Airflow UI or by downstream stakeholders noticing stale dashboards.

children.title: Airflow Params and DAG-level Variables
children.description: Airflow Params allow triggering a DAG with runtime inputs like a specific date range or model selector without modifying DAG code, enabling ad-hoc backfills through the UI. Hard-coding dates or environment names into DAG Python code instead of Params creates unparameterizable pipelines that require code changes for every backfill.

children.title: Sensor and External Task Dependencies
children.description: Airflow sensors pause execution until an external condition is true, such as a file arriving in S3 or an upstream DAG completing, without requiring the two DAGs to be merged. Polling with tight sensor intervals consumes scheduler resources, while missing sensors causes race conditions between ingestion and transformation.

analytics-engineer.title: Semantic Layer and Metric Definitions
analytics-engineer.description: Define business metrics centrally in the dbt semantic layer using MetricFlow so that every tool in the stack computes them consistently. Understand dimension and measure grain.
analytics-engineer.challenge: Define 5 company-wide metrics (revenue, CAC, LTV, churn rate, NPS proxy) using MetricFlow in a dbt project with correct grain and dimension configurations.

children.title: Defining Metrics with MetricFlow
children.description: MetricFlow replaces the deprecated dbt metrics spec with a YAML-based semantic model that defines measures, dimensions, and entities as first-class objects independent of any BI tool. Without MetricFlow, the same metric is computed differently in Looker LookML, Superset SQL, and ad-hoc notebook queries, producing contradictory numbers.

children.title: Semantic Models and Entities
children.description: Semantic models declare the grain of a table and label its columns as measures, dimensions, or join entities, creating a graph that MetricFlow traverses to construct correct SQL automatically. Incorrect entity declarations cause MetricFlow to generate queries that fan out on many-to-many joins, silently inflating every metric value.

children.title: Time Grain Configuration
children.description: Time grains control what date truncations MetricFlow allows for a metric, preventing nonsensical period breakdowns like weekly metrics being queried at hourly grain. Missing grain configuration lets consumers query at unsupported granularities, generating technically valid but business-meaningless results.

children.title: Ratio and Derived Metrics
children.description: Derived metrics compute expressions over other metrics (like conversion rate from clicks and sessions), and ratio metrics handle numerator-denominator pairs safely with correct NULL behavior. Computing ratios as a single simple metric instead of a ratio metric type causes division by zero errors and incorrect period filtering when dimensions are applied.

children.title: Centralized Metric Definitions
children.description: Storing all metric definitions in a version-controlled dbt project means every change is reviewed, tested, and auditable, creating a single source of truth for business KPIs. Spreadsheet-managed or BI-tool-embedded metric logic diverges silently across teams, causing the credibility crisis where no two reports agree.

children.title: Metric Filtering and Constraints
children.description: Metric-level filters in MetricFlow restrict the rows included in a measure calculation (e.g., only paid orders count toward revenue), encapsulating business rules that would otherwise be re-implemented inconsistently in every downstream query. Without embedded filters, consumers forget to apply required WHERE clauses and report inflated numbers.

children.title: Querying the Semantic Layer via API
children.description: The dbt Semantic Layer exposes a JDBC/ADBC API that BI tools and notebooks can query to retrieve metrics without writing raw SQL, ensuring the metric definition is always applied. Direct SQL bypassing the semantic layer ignores filters and grain constraints, returning numbers that contradict the official metric definition.

children.title: mf CLI for Metric Validation
children.description: The MetricFlow CLI (mf) lets you query and preview metric results locally before deploying, validating that grain, dimension, and filter configurations produce the expected SQL and row counts. Without local validation, semantic layer bugs only surface in production dashboards after deployment.

analytics-engineer.title: CI/CD and Analytics Engineering Best Practices
analytics-engineer.description: Apply software engineering practices to analytics code: version control, code review, automated testing in CI, and deployment pipelines. Ensure production dbt changes are safe and auditable.
analytics-engineer.challenge: Set up a GitHub Actions workflow that runs dbt test on modified models only (slim CI) on every pull request and blocks merge on test failure.

children.title: Git Branching Strategy for dbt
children.description: A clear branching model (feature branches, dev/staging/prod environments, protected main) ensures SQL changes are reviewed before reaching production models that power executive dashboards. Committing directly to main bypasses review and deploys untested logic instantly, risking silent data corruption.

children.title: Pull Request Reviews for SQL
children.description: SQL pull requests should verify model grain, join correctness, and test coverage in addition to syntax, because SQL logic errors do not produce compile errors that block merge. Reviewing only for style without checking the logic allows business-rule bugs to ship undetected.

resources.label: Code Review for Analytics - Locally Optimistic

children.title: Slim CI with State Comparison
children.description: dbt's --select state:modified+ flag runs only models changed in a pull request and their downstream dependents, making CI fast and cheap even in large projects. Running dbt build for all models on every PR wastes warehouse credits and creates CI queues that slow down the team.

children.title: dbt Deployment Environments
children.description: Separate dbt environments (dev, CI, staging, prod) with isolated warehouse schemas ensure that transformation experiments never overwrite production tables. Without environment separation, a developer running dbt in their terminal can overwrite production mart tables.

children.title: GitHub Actions Workflow for dbt
children.description: A GitHub Actions workflow can install dbt, run dbt build with a modified-state selector, and report test results as PR status checks, blocking merge on failures automatically. Without automated CI, test regressions are caught only after deployment to production, requiring manual rollbacks.

children.title: Secrets and Credentials in CI
children.description: Warehouse credentials and dbt Cloud API tokens must be stored as GitHub Actions secrets and injected as environment variables, never hard-coded in workflow YAML files. Hard-coded credentials in public repositories expose warehouse access to anyone with repository read access.

children.title: SQLFluff Linting
children.description: SQLFluff is a dialect-aware SQL linter that enforces style rules like consistent aliasing, join type declarations, and keyword casing across all dbt models in a project. Without automated linting, code review becomes a style argument and inconsistent SQL makes models harder to read and audit.

children.title: dbt pre-commit Hooks
children.description: Pre-commit hooks that run dbt compile, SQLFluff lint, and schema YAML validation on every commit catch errors locally before they reach the CI pipeline and slow down the team. Relying on CI alone for validation creates a slow feedback loop that discourages frequent commits.

analytics-engineer.title: Capstone - Production Analytics Platform
analytics-engineer.description: Build and deploy a production analytics engineering stack: dbt project with staging and mart layers, semantic layer metrics, Looker explores and dashboard, and Airflow orchestration. Document for team handoff.
analytics-engineer.challenge: Deliver a production-ready analytics project: dbt staging and 3 mart models with tests, 5 MetricFlow metrics, a Looker dashboard, an Airflow DAG, and a CI/CD pipeline on GitHub Actions.

children.title: Full Staging and Mart Layer Design
children.description: A production dbt project requires a complete layered architecture where staging models are one-to-one with sources, intermediate models handle complex joins, and mart models expose business-ready entities. Skipping the intermediate layer forces mart models to become unreadable multi-table joins that are hard to test and maintain.

resources.label: How We Structure Our dbt Projects

children.title: dbt Project Checklist for Production
children.description: A production-ready dbt project must include source freshness, model tests on all primary keys, documentation, environment-specific profiles, and a CI job before it is safe to hand off to a team. Projects without this foundation accumulate silent data quality debt that compounds over time.

children.title: Connecting Airflow, dbt, and Looker
children.description: A production analytics platform chains ingestion (Fivetran/Airbyte), transformation (dbt), and delivery (Looker) with Airflow as the orchestrator, so each layer waits for the previous one to succeed before proceeding. Without this end-to-end wiring, Looker dashboards can query dbt tables mid-run and display partially transformed data.

children.title: CI/CD Pipeline for the Full Stack
children.description: The capstone CI/CD pipeline must test dbt models on PR, run dbt Cloud production jobs on merge, and validate Looker LookML on changes, creating a fully automated path from code to dashboard. A manual deployment process at any stage breaks the safety guarantees that automated testing provides.

children.title: DataOps Principles for Analytics
children.description: DataOps applies DevOps principles to data pipelines: automated testing, version control, continuous delivery, and observability. Without DataOps practices, each deployment is a manual, high-risk event that the team dreads, slowing down the iteration cycle that keeps dashboards relevant.

children.title: Team Handoff Documentation
