children.description: Production analytics platforms require a README, a dbt project guide, environment setup instructions, and a runbook for common failure modes so new team members can operate and extend the system without the original author. Projects without handoff documentation create single-point-of-failure knowledge silos.

children.title: dbt Cloud Job Monitoring
children.description: dbt Cloud's run history, model-level timing, and test result dashboards provide observability into which models are slow, failing, or growing unexpectedly in row count. Without monitoring, performance regressions from new incremental models accumulate unnoticed until warehouse bills spike.

children.title: Data Observability Tools
children.description: Tools like Monte Carlo, Metaplan, or dbt's own anomaly detection packages monitor for row count drops, schema changes, and distribution shifts that tests cannot catch because they run only at pipeline execution time. Without observability, data incidents are discovered by stakeholders, not engineers.

statistician.title: Statistical Foundations and R Environment
statistician.description: Set up an R development environment and learn the foundational statistical concepts that underpin all quantitative analysis. Understand data types, probability, and the R language syntax.
statistician.challenge: Using base R, load a dataset, compute all descriptive statistics, produce a frequency table, and render a summary report with R Markdown.

children.title: Set Theory and Combinatorics
children.description: Probability is built on set theory — events are sets and operations like union and intersection define compound events. Without a firm grip on counting principles and sample spaces, calculating likelihoods of real outcomes becomes error-prone and unreliable.

children.title: Calculus for Probability
children.description: Continuous probability distributions require integration to compute areas under density curves, and derivatives appear in maximum likelihood estimation. Skipping this background leads to confusion when working with PDFs and CDFs in real models.

children.title: R Data Structures and Syntax
children.description: Vectors, data frames, lists, and factors are the native containers for statistical data in R. Misunderstanding how R recycles vectors or coerces types silently produces wrong results that are hard to detect downstream.

resources.label: R for Data Science: Data Structures

children.title: RStudio and Package Ecosystem
children.description: RStudio's project-based workflow, console, and built-in help system dramatically accelerate statistical work. Understanding how to install CRAN packages and manage environments with renv prevents reproducibility failures in collaborative projects.

children.title: Axioms and Rules of Probability
children.description: The three Kolmogorov axioms and derived rules — addition, multiplication, and complement — are the engine behind every probabilistic model. Misapplying the addition rule to non-mutually-exclusive events or the multiplication rule to dependent events introduces systematic bias.

children.title: Conditional Probability and Bayes Theorem
children.description: Conditional probability quantifies how new information updates our beliefs, and Bayes theorem formalizes that update. Confusing P(A|B) with P(B|A) — the prosecutor's fallacy — is one of the most common and consequential errors in applied statistics.

children.title: NumPy and SciPy for Statistical Computing
children.description: NumPy provides efficient array operations that underpin nearly every Python statistics library, while SciPy wraps classical statistical routines. Without understanding array broadcasting and vectorization, code becomes slow and difficult to scale to large datasets.

children.title: Jupyter Notebooks for Reproducible Analysis
children.description: Jupyter notebooks interleave code, output, and narrative in a single document, making statistical analyses easier to audit and share. Running cells out of order is a frequent source of hidden state bugs that invalidate reported results.

statistician.title: Probability Distributions and Sampling
statistician.description: Master the probability distributions most commonly encountered in statistical practice and understand the central limit theorem. Simulate sampling distributions to build intuition.
statistician.challenge: Simulate the central limit theorem in R for 3 different population distributions, plotting sample mean distributions at n=5, 30, and 100.

children.title: Binomial and Bernoulli Distributions
children.description: The binomial distribution models the number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials and is foundational for quality control, A/B testing, and survey analysis. Applying it when trials are not independent or the success probability varies between trials produces badly calibrated predictions.

children.title: Poisson and Negative Binomial Distributions
children.description: The Poisson distribution describes rare event counts per interval and is widely used in operations research, epidemiology, and web analytics. When variance substantially exceeds the mean — overdispersion — the Poisson assumption breaks down and the negative binomial is required.

children.title: Normal Distribution and Central Limit Theorem
children.description: The normal distribution's bell curve shape and the CLT — which states sample means converge to normality regardless of the population shape — justify most classical inference procedures. Assuming normality without checking can invalidate confidence intervals and p-values when sample sizes are small.

children.title: Exponential, Gamma, and Beta Distributions
children.description: The exponential distribution models waiting times between events, the gamma generalizes it to arbitrary shapes, and the beta constrains values to (0,1) making it ideal for modeling proportions and probabilities. Choosing the wrong distribution family for a response variable introduces structural bias that no amount of data can fix.

children.title: Sampling Methods and Bias
children.description: Simple random, stratified, cluster, and systematic sampling each have different variance properties and applicability. Convenience sampling and non-response bias can make even large samples unrepresentative, rendering statistical inferences meaningless.

children.title: Bootstrap and Resampling Methods
children.description: The bootstrap creates an empirical distribution of a statistic by repeatedly resampling with replacement, enabling confidence intervals without distributional assumptions. Ignoring the need for stratified bootstrapping in imbalanced data or clustered samples can produce overly narrow intervals.

children.title: Goodness-of-Fit Tests
children.description: Chi-square, Kolmogorov-Smirnov, and Anderson-Darling tests assess whether observed data plausibly come from a hypothesized distribution. Skipping this check and assuming a distribution leads to downstream model misspecification that compounds through every subsequent analysis step.

children.title: QQ Plots and Visual Diagnostics
children.description: Quantile-quantile plots compare empirical quantiles against a theoretical distribution, revealing heavy tails, skewness, and multimodality that numerical tests may miss at moderate sample sizes. Relying solely on p-values from normality tests at large N rejects trivial deviations while missing practically important non-normality at small N.

statistician.title: Inferential Statistics and Hypothesis Testing
statistician.description: Apply inferential statistics to draw conclusions from sample data and test business and scientific hypotheses rigorously. Control Type I and Type II errors with appropriate test selection.
statistician.challenge: Given a clinical trial dataset, select the appropriate test, verify assumptions, run the test in R, compute effect size (Cohen d), and write a formal conclusion.

children.title: Measures of Central Tendency and Spread
children.description: Mean, median, mode, variance, standard deviation, and IQR summarize the center and dispersion of a distribution. Reporting only the mean without spread — or using mean instead of median for skewed distributions like incomes — systematically misrepresents the data.

children.title: Skewness, Kurtosis, and Moments
children.description: Higher-order moments quantify asymmetry and tail heaviness beyond what mean and variance capture. Ignoring heavy tails in financial or operational data leads to catastrophic underestimation of extreme-event probabilities.

resources.label: Statistics by Jim: Skewness and Kurtosis

children.title: Histograms, Boxplots, and Violin Plots
children.description: Histograms reveal the full shape of a distribution, boxplots highlight outliers and quartiles, and violin plots combine both with a density estimate. Choosing the wrong bin width in a histogram or ignoring violin plots for bimodal data can make very different distributions look identical.

children.title: Correlation Matrices and Scatter Plots
children.description: Scatter plots expose nonlinear relationships and outliers that correlation coefficients summarize away, and correlation matrices reveal multicollinearity in predictors. Relying on Pearson r for non-linear or non-monotonic associations produces misleading conclusions about variable relationships.

children.title: Missing Data Mechanisms and Imputation
children.description: Data can be missing completely at random (MCAR), at random (MAR), or not at random (MNAR), and the correct handling differs in each case. Simply dropping rows with missing values when data is MNAR introduces selection bias that distorts every downstream estimate.

children.title: Outlier Detection and Treatment
children.description: Outliers can be genuine extreme values, measurement errors, or data entry mistakes, and each requires a different response. Blindly removing statistical outliers without domain justification discards real signal and inflates apparent model fit.

children.title: Categorical Data Encoding and Analysis
children.description: Nominal and ordinal categories require different encoding schemes — one-hot encoding, label encoding, or contrast coding — and misspecification propagates through regression and clustering models. Treating ordered categories as numeric without checking proportionality of spacing can severely distort model coefficients.

children.title: Tidy Data Principles
children.description: Hadley Wickham's tidy data framework — one observation per row, one variable per column, one type per table — makes data manipulation, visualization, and modeling dramatically more reliable. Violating tidy principles leads to implicit data duplication and ambiguous join keys that produce incorrect aggregations.

statistician.title: Regression Analysis
statistician.description: Build and interpret simple and multiple regression models, checking all assumptions rigorously. Apply transformations and diagnostics to handle violations.
statistician.challenge: Fit a multiple regression model in R, run all 4 LINE assumption checks with plots, identify and handle two influential observations, and interpret coefficients with 95% CIs.

children.title: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Error Types
children.description: Every test is a decision between a null hypothesis of no effect and an alternative, with Type I error (false positive) controlled by alpha and Type II error (false negative) depending on power. Conflating statistical significance with practical significance — or setting alpha without accounting for multiple testing — leads to false discoveries or missed real effects.

children.title: p-values, Confidence Intervals, and Effect Sizes
children.description: A p-value measures the probability of observed data under the null, not the probability the null is true, and confidence intervals quantify estimation uncertainty. Reporting only p-values without effect sizes and CIs obscures whether a statistically significant result is practically meaningful.

children.title: t-Tests: One-sample, Two-sample, Paired
children.description: t-tests compare means under normality and either equal or unequal variance assumptions, with Welch's t-test being more robust when variances differ. Using a paired test on independent samples or vice versa dramatically inflates or deflates the standard error and invalidates the p-value.

children.title: Chi-Square Tests for Association and Goodness-of-Fit
children.description: The chi-square test of independence detects associations between categorical variables, and the goodness-of-fit variant tests whether observed frequencies match expected ones. Applying the chi-square test when expected cell counts are below 5 violates its asymptotic assumptions and requires Fisher's exact test instead.

children.title: Mann-Whitney U, Wilcoxon, and Kruskal-Wallis Tests
children.description: Non-parametric rank-based tests compare distributions without assuming normality, making them robust for ordinal data, small samples, and heavy-tailed distributions. They test distributional differences broadly, not just means, so interpreting them as mean comparisons can be misleading when shapes differ.

children.title: Multiple Testing Corrections
children.description: Testing many hypotheses simultaneously inflates the family-wise error rate; Bonferroni correction is conservative while Benjamini-Hochberg controls the false discovery rate more leniently. Ignoring multiple comparisons in genomics, clinical trials, or large-scale A/B tests guarantees spurious discoveries in the reported results.

resources.label: Nature Methods: Points of Significance — Multiple Testing

children.title: Power Analysis and Sample Size Determination
children.description: Power analysis determines the minimum sample size to detect a given effect at specified alpha and power levels, preventing underpowered studies that cannot detect real effects. Running a study without a prior power calculation often results in ambiguous null findings that neither confirm nor rule out the effect of interest.

children.title: Cohen's d and Practical Significance
children.description: Cohen's d standardizes the mean difference by the pooled standard deviation, providing an effect size that is comparable across studies with different scales and sample sizes. A tiny but statistically significant effect in a large dataset often has no actionable practical importance, making effect size reporting essential.

statistician.title: Experimental Design and A/B Testing
statistician.description: Design statistically valid experiments and analyze results with appropriate tests. Compute required sample sizes and manage multiple comparison problems.
statistician.challenge: Design an A/B test for a website conversion experiment: compute required sample size for 80% power at alpha=0.05, describe the randomization strategy, and simulate the analysis.

children.title: OLS Assumptions and Diagnostics
children.description: Ordinary least squares regression requires linearity, independence, homoscedasticity, and normally distributed errors; violating these assumptions biases coefficients or invalidates standard errors. Skipping residual plots and influence diagnostics leaves violations undetected, making every inference from the model unreliable.

children.title: Multicollinearity and VIF
children.description: Multicollinearity inflates the variance of coefficient estimates, making them unstable and difficult to interpret, with the variance inflation factor (VIF) quantifying the severity. Ignoring high VIF values leads to models where individually insignificant predictors mask a jointly strong effect, and small data changes flip coefficient signs.

children.title: Logistic Regression
children.description: Logistic regression models the log-odds of a binary outcome as a linear combination of predictors, requiring separation checks and calibration evaluation beyond accuracy metrics. Treating probability outputs as calibrated without a calibration plot leads to wrong risk assessments in medical or credit scoring applications.

children.title: Poisson and Negative Binomial Regression
children.description: Count data with non-negative integer outcomes calls for Poisson or negative binomial GLMs rather than OLS, since linear regression produces non-integer predictions and can estimate negative counts. Ignoring overdispersion and using Poisson when the variance exceeds the mean produces underestimated standard errors and falsely precise inference.

children.title: AIC, BIC, and Cross-validation
children.description: AIC and BIC penalize model complexity while rewarding fit, providing principled model comparison without a held-out test set, while cross-validation directly estimates predictive performance. Selecting the model with the lowest training error without penalizing complexity guarantees overfitting that degrades real-world prediction.

children.title: Regularization: Ridge and Lasso
children.description: Ridge regression shrinks all coefficients toward zero to reduce variance in the presence of multicollinearity, while Lasso performs variable selection by setting some coefficients exactly to zero. Applying no regularization in high-dimensional settings with more predictors than observations leads to an underdetermined system with infinite solutions.

children.title: Residual Analysis and Influential Points
children.description: Cook's distance, leverage, and DFFITS identify observations that disproportionately influence fitted values or coefficient estimates. A single high-leverage outlier can shift the regression line substantially, making the model fit the outlier at the expense of the majority of the data.

children.title: Heteroscedasticity Tests
children.description: Breusch-Pagan and White tests formally check whether residual variance changes with fitted values or predictors, a violation that makes OLS standard errors incorrect. Presenting OLS standard errors under heteroscedasticity overstates precision; robust (HC) standard errors are required for valid inference.

statistician.title: Bayesian Statistics
statistician.description: Apply Bayesian reasoning to statistical inference, expressing uncertainty through posterior distributions rather than binary reject/fail-to-reject decisions. Use MCMC sampling for complex models.
statistician.challenge: Implement a Bayesian A/B test in PyMC comparing two conversion rates, plot the posterior distributions, and compute the probability that variant B is better than A.

children.title: One-way and Two-way ANOVA
children.description: ANOVA partitions total variance into between-group and within-group components to test whether group means differ, with two-way ANOVA additionally capturing interaction effects between factors. Interpreting main effects in the presence of a significant interaction without examining the interaction plot leads to contradictory and incorrect conclusions.

children.title: Post-hoc Tests: Tukey HSD, Bonferroni
children.description: A significant ANOVA F-test only establishes that at least one group mean differs; post-hoc tests identify which specific pairs differ while controlling the family-wise error rate. Running all pairwise t-tests without correction inflates Type I error by a factor proportional to the number of comparisons.

children.title: Randomization, Blocking, and Control Groups
children.description: Randomization eliminates systematic confounding, blocking reduces variance by grouping similar units before assignment, and control groups provide the counterfactual baseline. Without proper randomization, observed differences between treatment groups may be entirely attributable to pre-existing differences rather than the treatment.

children.title: Factorial and Fractional Factorial Designs
children.description: Full factorial designs estimate all main effects and interactions between multiple factors, while fractional factorials trade resolution for fewer runs when resources are constrained. Confounding high-order interactions in a fractional design without understanding the alias structure contaminates main effect estimates.

children.title: Sequential Testing and Stopping Rules
children.description: Peeking at test results repeatedly and stopping as soon as p < 0.05 is reached — without pre-registered stopping rules — inflates Type I error to as high as 30% for a nominal 5% test. Sequential testing frameworks like the sequential probability ratio test or always-valid p-values maintain error control under continuous monitoring.

children.title: Metric Selection and Guardrail Metrics
children.description: Primary metrics should be sensitive to the treatment and aligned with business goals, while guardrail metrics protect against regressions in key areas like latency or revenue. Optimizing for a single metric without guardrails produces treatments that win on one dimension while silently degrading others.

children.title: Normality and Homogeneity of Variance
children.description: ANOVA assumes residuals are normally distributed and that group variances are equal (homoscedasticity), verified through Shapiro-Wilk and Levene's tests respectively. Violating homogeneity of variance in unbalanced designs biases the F-test substantially; Welch's ANOVA provides a robust alternative.

children.title: Repeated Measures ANOVA and Sphericity
children.description: Repeated measures ANOVA accounts for within-subject correlation when the same units are measured under multiple conditions, but requires the sphericity assumption that variance of differences between all pairs of conditions is equal. Violating sphericity without applying Greenhouse-Geisser or Huynh-Feldt correction inflates Type I error in the omnibus test.

statistician.title: Multivariate Statistics
statistician.description: Apply multivariate methods to datasets with many variables, reducing dimensionality and uncovering latent structure. Use PCA, factor analysis, and MANOVA for statistical analysis.
statistician.challenge: Apply PCA to a high-dimensional dataset in R, determine the number of components to retain using a scree plot and Kaiser rule, and interpret the first 3 principal components.

children.title: Prior Distributions and Prior Elicitation
children.description: A prior encodes beliefs about parameters before observing data; informative priors accelerate learning but can dominate the posterior when data are sparse. Choosing an improper or inadvertently highly informative prior without sensitivity analysis can produce posteriors that reflect the prior more than the data.

children.title: Likelihood and Posterior Computation
children.description: The posterior distribution is proportional to the likelihood times the prior, and is the complete Bayesian answer to an inference question. Failing to verify that the MCMC sampler has converged means the posterior samples do not represent the true posterior, invalidating credible intervals and predictions.

children.title: Metropolis-Hastings and Gibbs Sampling
children.description: Metropolis-Hastings proposes new parameter values and accepts or rejects them based on the posterior ratio, while Gibbs sampling cycles through full conditional distributions when they are available in closed form. Poor proposal distributions in Metropolis-Hastings lead to very low acceptance rates and slow mixing, yielding biased posterior estimates.

resources.label: Probabilistic Programming and Bayesian Methods for Hackers

children.title: Convergence Diagnostics: R-hat and ESS
children.description: The R-hat statistic compares within-chain and between-chain variance to detect non-convergence, and effective sample size (ESS) measures how much independent information the correlated MCMC draws contain. Running inference with R-hat above 1.01 or low ESS produces unreliable posterior summaries that can mislead downstream decisions.

children.title: Credible Intervals vs. Confidence Intervals
children.description: A Bayesian credible interval directly states there is a 95% probability the parameter lies within it given the data and prior, whereas a frequentist confidence interval has a more indirect interpretation about repeated sampling. Communicating a confidence interval as if it were a credible interval is one of the most widespread misinterpretations in applied statistics.

children.title: Bayes Factors and Model Comparison
children.description: Bayes factors quantify the relative evidence for two models by computing the ratio of their marginal likelihoods, providing a continuous scale of evidence rather than a binary decision. Interpreting Bayes factors without accounting for the sensitivity to prior specification can make weakly informative priors yield misleadingly strong evidence.

children.title: PyMC and Stan for Hierarchical Models
children.description: Hierarchical (multilevel) models share statistical strength across groups by treating group-level parameters as drawn from a common distribution, and PyMC and Stan provide flexible interfaces to specify them. Ignoring hierarchical structure in nested data — students within schools, patients within hospitals — leads to either ignoring group variation or overfitting individual groups.

children.title: Posterior Predictive Checks
children.description: Posterior predictive checks simulate new data from the fitted model and compare them to observed data to detect systematic model failures. Skipping this step means a model that converged and has good MCMC diagnostics may still be badly misspecified in ways that invalidate predictions and decisions.

statistician.title: Time Series and Forecasting
statistician.description: Analyze time-indexed data using classical and modern methods, accounting for trend, seasonality, and autocorrelation. Produce forecasts with formal uncertainty quantification.
statistician.challenge: Fit competing ARIMA and ETS models to a quarterly economic time series in R, compare AIC and out-of-sample MASE, and produce a 4-quarter forecast with 80% and 95% prediction intervals.

children.title: Stationarity and Unit Root Tests
children.description: A stationary time series has constant mean, variance, and autocovariance over time, which is a prerequisite for most classical time series models. Fitting an ARIMA model to a non-stationary series without differencing produces spurious regressions where unrelated trending series appear strongly correlated.

resources.label: Towards Data Science: Stationarity in Time Series

children.title: ACF, PACF, and ARIMA Model Identification
children.description: The autocorrelation function and partial autocorrelation function plots guide selection of the AR and MA orders in ARIMA models by revealing lag structure in the series. Misreading the cutoff patterns in ACF/PACF leads to over- or under-differenced models that either fail to capture the dynamics or introduce unnecessary complexity.

children.title: Exponential Smoothing and ETS Models
children.description: Exponential smoothing assigns exponentially decreasing weights to past observations and ETS models decompose series into error, trend, and seasonal components with additive or multiplicative structures. Applying additive seasonality to a series with multiplicative seasonality — where amplitude grows with level — systematically underpredicts peaks.

children.title: SARIMA and Seasonal Decomposition
children.description: SARIMA extends ARIMA with seasonal AR and MA terms to capture periodic patterns at fixed lags, while STL decomposition robustly separates trend, seasonality, and remainder components. Ignoring seasonality in retail, energy, or climate data produces forecasts that are systematically wrong at every seasonal peak and trough.

children.title: Cross-validation for Time Series
children.description: Standard k-fold cross-validation shuffles data, but time series require time-ordered splits — rolling or expanding windows — to avoid using future information to predict the past. Using shuffled cross-validation on time series data produces optimistic error estimates because it effectively allows the model to learn from the future.

children.title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics: MAE, RMSE, MAPE
children.description: MAE is robust to outliers, RMSE penalizes large errors more heavily, and MAPE expresses errors as a percentage but is undefined at zero and biased toward over-forecasting. Choosing MAPE as the sole metric for demand forecasting with intermittent demand leads to unstable and misleading scores.

children.title: Cointegration and Vector Autoregression
children.description: Cointegrated series share a long-run equilibrium relationship even if individually non-stationary, and VAR models capture dynamic interdependencies among multiple time series simultaneously. Differencing cointegrated series to achieve stationarity discards the long-run relationship information and leads to misspecified models.

children.title: Prophet and Neural Forecasting
children.description: Meta's Prophet decomposes time series into trend, seasonality, and holiday effects with an intuitive interface, while neural methods like N-BEATS capture complex nonlinear patterns. Treating Prophet as a black box without inspecting its component plots leads to missed trend changepoints or spurious seasonality that degrades accuracy.

statistician.title: Survival Analysis and Specialized Methods
statistician.description: Apply survival analysis to time-to-event data and extend statistical skills to generalized linear models for non-normal outcomes. Handle censored data correctly.
statistician.challenge: Analyze a customer churn dataset as a survival problem: fit Kaplan-Meier curves by segment, test for differences with a log-rank test, and fit a Cox model with 3 covariates.

children.title: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
children.description: PCA rotates the data into orthogonal directions of maximum variance, reducing dimensionality while retaining most of the information measured by variance. Applying PCA to unstandardized variables with very different scales causes high-variance variables to dominate all principal components, obscuring the true structure.

children.title: Factor Analysis and Structural Interpretation
children.description: Exploratory factor analysis seeks latent variables that explain observed correlations, unlike PCA which is purely a variance decomposition, making it appropriate when the goal is to discover theoretical constructs. Retaining too many or too few factors — without using scree plots, parallel analysis, or interpretability criteria — produces components that are either noisy or incomplete.

children.title: MANOVA and Hotelling's T-squared
children.description: MANOVA extends ANOVA to multiple dependent variables simultaneously, testing whether group centroids differ in multivariate space, with Hotelling's T-squared as the two-group special case. Running separate ANOVAs for each outcome variable inflates Type I error and ignores the covariance structure among outcomes.

children.title: Canonical Correlation Analysis
children.description: CCA finds linear combinations of two variable sets that maximize their mutual correlation, revealing the underlying relationships between two multivariate domains such as psychological tests and academic outcomes. Interpreting canonical variates without examining the canonical loadings alongside the correlations produces conclusions about variable importance that the analysis does not support.

children.title: K-means and Cluster Validation
children.description: K-means partitions data into k clusters by minimizing within-cluster sum of squares, but requires specifying k in advance and is sensitive to outliers and cluster shape. Choosing k by visual inspection alone without silhouette scores, the elbow method, or gap statistic produces arbitrary partitions that do not reflect real data structure.

children.title: Hierarchical Clustering and Dendrograms
children.description: Agglomerative hierarchical clustering builds a tree of nested clusters using linkage criteria such as Ward, complete, or average linkage, visualized as a dendrogram. Using single linkage in the presence of elongated clusters produces the chaining effect where clusters grow by absorbing isolated points rather than cohesive groups.

children.title: Covariance and Correlation Matrices
children.description: The covariance matrix encodes all pairwise linear relationships and is the foundation for PCA, factor analysis, MANOVA, and multivariate normal distributions. Using the sample covariance matrix in high dimensions (p close to n) produces a poorly conditioned estimate; shrinkage estimators like Ledoit-Wolf are required for reliable results.

children.title: Mahalanobis Distance and Outlier Detection
children.description: Mahalanobis distance scales Euclidean distance by the inverse covariance matrix, making it scale-invariant and accounting for correlations between variables when identifying multivariate outliers. Using Euclidean distance for outlier detection in correlated high-dimensional data incorrectly flags observations that are unusual only on one axis but typical in the full multivariate sense.

statistician.title: Capstone - Statistical Consulting Project
statistician.description: Deliver a complete statistical analysis project mimicking a consulting engagement: define the research question, select methods, conduct analysis, validate assumptions, and present findings in a formal statistical report.
statistician.challenge: Complete a statistical consulting-style project: analyze a real dataset, apply at least 3 statistical methods, validate all assumptions, produce a 10-page formal statistical report, and present findings to a non-technical audience.

children.title: tidyverse for Data Manipulation
children.description: The tidyverse — dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2, purrr — provides a consistent grammar for data manipulation and visualization that dramatically reduces code complexity and bugs. Mixing base R and tidyverse idioms inconsistently in the same pipeline often produces subtle class-coercion errors that break downstream statistical functions.

children.title: ggplot2 for Statistical Graphics
children.description: ggplot2 implements Wilkinson's Grammar of Graphics, allowing complex multi-layered visualizations to be built from reusable components. Ignoring scale transformations and coordinate systems when plotting skewed distributions misleads viewers about the true shape and spread of the data.

children.title: statsmodels for Classical Statistics
children.description: statsmodels provides classical econometric and statistical models with full summary tables, confidence intervals, and diagnostic tests that scikit-learn deliberately omits for its prediction-focused API. Using scikit-learn for inference problems because of its familiar API produces models without valid p-values, standard errors, or diagnostic outputs.

children.title: Pandas for Data Wrangling
children.description: Pandas DataFrames are the primary data container for Python statistical workflows, supporting groupby operations, merges, reshaping, and time series indexing. Misunderstanding how Pandas handles chained indexing and copy-on-write semantics leads to SettingWithCopyWarning and silent failures to update data.

children.title: R Markdown and Quarto for Reproducible Reports
children.description: R Markdown and Quarto interweave code, results, and prose in a single document that re-executes on demand, eliminating copy-paste errors between analysis and report. Manually copying tables and figures into documents breaks reproducibility and frequently introduces numerical discrepancies between reported and computed results.

children.title: Version Control for Statistical Projects
children.description: Git tracks changes to analysis scripts, enables collaboration, and provides an audit trail for regulatory or peer-review purposes. Storing only final outputs without versioning analysis code makes it impossible to reproduce or audit earlier results when methodology questions arise.

children.title: Vectorization and NumPy Performance
children.description: Replacing Python loops with NumPy vectorized operations typically yields 10-100x speedups because NumPy delegates computation to optimized C and Fortran routines. Writing element-wise Python loops over large arrays in a statistical pipeline makes interactive analysis impractically slow on realistic dataset sizes.

children.title: Parallel Computing with R (parallel, future)
children.description: The parallel and future packages in R allow embarrassingly parallel tasks — bootstrap replicates, cross-validation folds, simulation runs — to use all available CPU cores. Running thousands of simulation iterations sequentially when the tasks are independent wastes wall-clock time and makes large-scale Monte Carlo studies infeasible.
